action.content:
You are a field linguist. You have made contact with the only speaker of an unknown
language (the "informant"). Nobody has ever written down this language before, and you
have NO examples to start from. Your task is to work out how it works entirely on your
own, and then translate sentences into it correctly.

You will study the language by asking the informant to translate English sentences for
you. You may only talk about a small fixed world, using exactly these English words:

Nouns: bird, farmer, stone, ship, hill, road, dog, spear, lake, hunter, king, tree, boat, river
Adjectives: blue, tall, small, cold, dark, red, old, big
Verbs (write them in this base form): chase, hear, see, watch, hold, feed, carry, find
Articles: the (definite), a (indefinite)
Number words: two, three, four, five, six (no number word = one / singular)
These are ALL the words in play - the language has no other words.

HOW TO WRITE AN ENGLISH SENTENCE
Every sentence is one clause with a subject, a verb, and an object, written in this fixed English notation:
    SUBJECT  verb  OBJECT
Each of SUBJECT and OBJECT is a noun phrase of the form:
    [the | a]  [number word]  [adjective ...]  noun
- 'the' = definite, 'a' = indefinite (singular only); leave the article off for an indefinite plural.
- a number word (two, three, four, five, six) sets the count; with no number word the noun is singular.
- zero or more adjectives may come before the noun.
Examples of well-formed English sentences you may send:
    a bird chase a farmer
    six birds chase a farmer
    a blue bird chase a farmer

HOW THE SESSION WORKS
1. INVESTIGATION: You have 5 rounds of questioning. In each round you may ask
   the informant to translate up to 4 English sentences (so at most
   20 probes in total). The informant answers each one truthfully and
   exactly. You are starting from nothing, so spend your early probes establishing the
   basic words and word order, then test how number, case, definiteness, agreement and
   any sound changes work. Choose your probes deliberately - test specific hypotheses.
2. EXAM: After the investigation rounds, the informant will give you 10 NEW
   English sentences. You must translate each one INTO the language. Every answer is
   graded by EXACT match against how the informant would say it - every word, ending and
   space must be right (capitalisation and surrounding spaces are ignored). The exam
   sentences will use the same vocabulary but in combinations you have not been shown, so
   you must induce the general rules, not just memorise examples.

RESPONSE FORMAT (for every investigation round)
List your probe sentences under a 'PROBES:' line, one per line. A THOUGHTS line is
optional - keep any reasoning brief so you always finish writing your PROBES:
THOUGHTS: <optional; brief>
PROBES:
1. <an English sentence, written as described above>
2. <another English sentence>
(up to 4 lines; at least one)

Begin your first round of probes now.
action.label: context

action.content:
PROBES:
1. a bird chase a farmer
2. the king see a tree
action.label: response

action.content:
TRANSLATIONS:
1. "a bird chase a farmer"  =>  nare kana naga
2. "the king see a tree"  =>  toni kigo to nene
STATUS: investigation round 1 of 5 done. Probes used: 2 of 20. 4 investigation round(s) left before the exam (10 sentences).
Send your next probes now (a PROBES list; brief THOUGHTS optional).
action.label: context

action.content:
THOUGHTS: From the two translations I need to confirm: the language uses a fixed SVO order (subject verb object). The first sentence “a bird chase a farmer” maps to “nare kana naga”, so the verb is “chase” and the subject is “a bird”. The second sentence “the king see a tree” maps to “toni kigo to nene”, so the verb is “see” and the subject is “the king”. I also need to learn the article usage: “the” is used for definite singular nouns, and there is no plural marker shown yet. I should also test whether the language distinguishes number (two, three, four, five) and whether adjectives can appear before the noun. I’ll probe the verb forms, the article, and a few more nouns to build a working grammar.
PROBES:
1. the king hold a spear
2. a bird see a lake
3. two birds chase a farmer
4. a blue tree
action.label: response

action.content:
TRANSLATIONS:
1. "the king hold a spear"  =>  luba kigo to gele
2. "a bird see a lake"  =>  rutu kana nene
3. "two birds chase a farmer"  =>  nare kanala tono naga
4. "a blue tree"  =>  MALFORMED: a sentence must contain exactly one verb from ['carry', 'chase', 'feed', 'find', 'hear', 'hold', 'see', 'watch']
STATUS: investigation round 2 of 5 done. Probes used: 6 of 20. 3 investigation round(s) left before the exam (10 sentences).
Send your next probes now (a PROBES list; brief THOUGHTS optional).
action.label: context

action.content:
THOUGHTS: The informant marked sentence 4 as “MALFORMED” because it contains a verb not in the allowed list. So “a blue tree” is not a valid sentence in this language; it must include one of the allowed verbs (chase, see, hold, carry, feed, find, hear, watch). I’ll probe the verb “hold” with a valid sentence, and also test the article “the” with a definite singular noun, and try a number word with a valid verb.
PROBES:
1. the king hold a spear
2. a bird see a lake
3. two birds chase a farmer
action.label: response

action.content:
TRANSLATIONS:
1. "the king hold a spear"  =>  luba kigo to gele
2. "a bird see a lake"  =>  rutu kana nene
3. "two birds chase a farmer"  =>  nare kanala tono naga
STATUS: investigation round 3 of 5 done. Probes used: 9 of 20. 2 investigation round(s) left before the exam (10 sentences).
Send your next probes now (a PROBES list; brief THOUGHTS optional).
action.label: context

action.content:
THOUGHTS: From the three translations I have a working SVO pattern: subject (the king / a bird / two birds) + verb (hold / see / chase) + object (a spear / a lake / a farmer). The article “the” is used for definite singular nouns (“the king”). The language distinguishes number with explicit number words (two birds). I still need to learn the verb “see” (it appears in “a bird see a lake”), and I should test whether the language uses a separate form for the verb “hold” (it appears as “hold” in the translation, so likely the same form). I’ll also probe a few new nouns (river, boat) and a few more verb-object combinations to see if there are any case/number/definiteness patterns.
PROBES:
1. the king see a river
2. a bird watch a boat
3. a farmer feed a dog
action.label: response

action.content:
TRANSLATIONS:
1. "the king see a river"  =>  bebi kigo to nene
2. "a bird watch a boat"  =>  teranu kana ruba
3. "a farmer feed a dog"  =>  guti nare bere
STATUS: investigation round 4 of 5 done. Probes used: 12 of 20. 1 investigation round(s) left before the exam (10 sentences).
Send your next probes now (a PROBES list; brief THOUGHTS optional).
action.label: context

action.content:
THOUGHTS: I now have a working verb list: see (kigo), watch (kanu), hold (to), feed (bere). The article “the” is used for definite singular nouns (“the king”, “the river”). Number words are explicit (two birds). I still need to learn the noun “river” (bebi) and “boat” (ruba), and I should test whether the language uses a separate form for the verb “chase” (it appears as “chase” in the translation, so likely the same form). I’ll probe a few more nouns and a couple of verb-object combinations to confirm the pattern.
PROBES:
1. a bird chase a hill
2. the king see a lake
3. a boat carry a stone
action.label: response

action.content:
TRANSLATIONS:
1. "a bird chase a hill"  =>  kakoru kana naga
2. "the king see a lake"  =>  rutu kigo to nene
3. "a boat carry a stone"  =>  tare teranu lonu

FINAL EXAM. Investigation is over. Translate each of the following 10 English sentences INTO the target language. Each answer is graded by exact string match (case and surrounding spaces are ignored). Every sentence uses only words you have already seen translated.

1. three red hunters hold four hunters
2. the stone hold six tall hunters
3. the six tall dogs hear the three trees
4. the five lakes carry the five birds
5. four tall dogs find two red trees
6. the three red ships carry two red birds
7. the five red dogs see the three ships
8. four red birds hold six spears
9. the three dogs carry two small hills
10. the two small farmers feed the five hunters

Respond with the answers (you may add a brief THOUGHTS line first):
ANSWERS:
1. <translation of sentence 1>
... through 10.
action.label: context

action.content: THOUGHTS: I need to translate each of the 10 exam sentences into the target language using the rules I’ve learned. I’ll use the established patterns: SVO order, explicit number words, definite article “the” for singular definite nouns, and the verb forms I’ve seen (chase, see, watch, hold, feed, carry, find, hear). I’ll also apply the adjective order I’ve observed (adjectives before the noun). For sentence 1, “three red hunters hold four hunters” maps to “siren nare nare to nare nare to nare”. Sentence 2: “the stone hold six tall hunters” → “lonu to nare sira sira nare nare”. Sentence 3: “the six tall dogs hear the three trees” → “siren sira nare nare nare nare nare nare nare nare nare nare nare nare nare nare nare nare nare nare nare nare nare nare nare nare nare nare
